final_answer:
Industrialization’s intense surge fundamentally altered Europe’s social and economic fabrics. A massive shift occurred: many persons left rural farmland for urban factories. This generated enormous needs for labor, drawing in populations previously tied to agrarian customs.

Economically, a new system emerged. Capital, previously dispersed, became concentrated in the hands of a powerful class of owners and entrepreneurs. This powerful segment generated unprecedented wealth, driving massive growth and production. Factories demanded disciplined, wage-based labor, effectively replacing the feudal or domestic systems.

Socially, this created extreme class stratification. We saw the intense development of a working class—the proletariat—whose lives were governed by harsh factory routines. In sharp contrast stood the bourgeoisie, the new moneyed elite. These diverse social elements fueled massive societal change, creating persistent tensions and compelling the rise of collective political movements seeking a fair distribution of wealth and humane working standards. Europe’s entire social makeup was irrevocably changed by this powerful, rapid transformation.<turn|>
